questions.text: The nucleus of Uranium-235 differs from a nucleus of a Uranium-238 in that Uranium-238 contains:

options.explanation: Here's correct calculation:N = A-Z = 235-92 = 143N = A-Z = 238-92 = 146
Number of electrons are same in both the uraniums that is 92.
Number of protons are same in both uraniums that is 92.

questions.explanation: Uranium-235 and Uranium-238 are isotopes, meaning they have the same number of protons (92) but differ in the number of neutrons. Uranium-238 has 238 - 92 = 146 neutrons, while Uranium-235 has 235 - 92 = 143 neutrons, so it has 3 more neutrons.

The number of neutrons is equal to the difference between the mass number of the atom (A) and the atomic number (Z).N = A-Z = 235-92 = 143

questions.text: What will happen in a time of 7 hours, if a radioactive substance has an average life of 7 hours?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Half of the active nuclei decay
options.explanation: This option suggests that the amount of decay is equivalent to the half-life, which is not the same as the average life.

options.text: Less than half of the active nuclei decay
options.explanation: This option incorrectly implies that the decay is less significant than what occurs over the average life, which is not the case.

options.text: More than half of the active nuclei decay
options.explanation: Since the average life of a radioactive substance is longer than its half-life, more than half of the active nuclei will decay in the time equivalent to the average life.
This option suggests complete decay, which is not possible in the time span of one average life.

questions.explanation: The average life of a radioactive substance is the reciprocal of the decay constant, and it is longer than the half-life. The half-life is the time taken for half of the radioactive nuclei to decay. In the given scenario, the time duration is equal to the average life, which means more than half of the radioactive nuclei would have decayed. Option C is correct because it accurately reflects the decay amount over the average life span. Option A is incorrect because it describes the half-life scenario, not the average life. Option B is incorrect as it underestimates the decay, and Option D is incorrect because not all nuclei will decay in this time frame.

questions.text: If 92U233 decays twice by α-emission, the resulting specie will be
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When 92U233 decays twice by α-emission, it loses two alpha particles, which each consist of two protons and two neutrons. This results in the loss of four protons and four neutrons from the original nucleus. The resulting isotopes will be 88Ra225, which has 88 protons and 225 nucleons (protons + neutrons), and 86Rn221, which has 86 protons and 221 nucleons.

questions.text: Radon-220 is radioactive and decays to Polonium-216 with the emission of an α-particle. The equation for the radioactive decay is shown as 86220Rn→84216Po + 24He. How many neutrons are in the Radon and Polonium nuclei?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: 86 is the proton number of Rn and 84 is the proton number of Po. This option does not give us a correct number of the neutron of both the elements. Hence, this option is incorrect.

In the case of Radon-220, the atomic number is 86, which means it has 86 protons. The mass number is 220, which means it has 220 nucleons (protons + neutrons). Therefore, the number of neutrons in Radon-220 is 220 - 86 = 134.In the case of Polonium-216, the atomic number is 84, which means it has 84 protons. The mass number is 216, which means it has 216 nucleons (protons + neutrons). Therefore, the number of neutrons in Polonium-216 is 216 - 84 = 132.
220 is the mass number of Rn, not its neutron number. The second value given is also not the neutron number of Po as we do not get this value by calculations.

questions.text: The binding energy for nucleus A is 7.7 MeV and that for nucleus B is 7.8 MeV. Which nucleus has the larger mass?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: It is found that binding energy is more for heavier nuclei. In the given problem, binding energy is more for nucleus B, therefore, nucleus B has larger mass.Hence, this option is incorrect as it suggests otherwise.

It is found that binding energy is more for heavier nuclei. In the given problem binding energy is more for nucleus B, therefore, nucleus B has larger mass.Hence, this option is incorrect as it suggests otherwise.

Binding Energy is the minimum amount of energy required to break all the nucleons (neutrons + protons) from each other.The nucleus has inter-nuclear force between its protons and neutrons.It was experimentally seen that the mass of individual protons and neutrons is less than the total mass of the nucleus. This mass gap is known as “mass defect”.This mass Defect is released in the form of energy, known as Binding Energy.Also, the Binding Energy of the nucleus is directly proportional to the mass of the nucleus.Here in the question, the Nucleus of B has high Binding Energy therefore, B has the larger Mass.

It is found that binding energy is more for heavier nuclei. In the given problem binding energy is more for nucleus B, therefore, nucleus B has larger mass.Hence, this option is incorrect as it suggests otherwise.

questions.explanation: Binding Energy is the minimum amount of energy required to break all the nucleons (neutrons + protons) from each other.The nucleus has a very strong inter-nuclear force between its protons and neutrons.It was experimentally seen that the mass of individual protons and neutrons is less than the total mass of the nucleus. This mass gap is known as 'Mass Defect'.This 'Mass Defect' is released in the form of energy, known as 'Binding Energy'.Also, the binding energy of the nucleus is directly proportional to the mass of the nucleus.Here in the question, the Nucleus of B has high binding energy therefore, B has the larger Mass.

questions.text: Which of the following particles has the highest value of mass?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Photons are massless particles. They do not have any rest mass, and their energy is solely defined by their frequency and wavelength.

Leptons are a family of elementary particles that includes electrons, muons, and neutrinos, among others. While leptons do have mass, their masses are much smaller than those of baryons and mesons. Among leptons, neutrinos were initially believed to be massless, but recent experiments have shown that they have extremely small masses.
Mesons are composite particles made up of a quark and an antiquark. They are more massive than leptons but still generally lighter than baryons.

Baryons are also composite particles and include protons and neutrons. They consist of three quarks (or three antiquarks). Due to their greater number of constituent quarks, baryons are generally much more massive than leptons and mesons.

questions.explanation: Baryons are also composite particles and include protons and neutrons. They consist of three quarks (or three antiquarks). Due to their greater number of constituent quarks, baryons are generally much more massive than leptons and mesons.

questions.text: When a radioactive element emits a β-particle, the mass number of the atom will be:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A beta particle is an electron, and it has no mass. The only thing that changes when a beta particle is emitted is the atomic number of the atom.Thus, this option is incorrect.
A beta particle is an electron, and it has no mass. The only thing that changes when a beta particle is emitted is the atomic number of the atom.Thus, this option is incorrect.
A beta particle is an electron, and it has no mass. The only thing that changes when a beta particle is emitted is the atomic number of the atom.Thus, this option is incorrect.

When a radioactive element emits a beta particle, the mass number of the atom will remain the same. A beta particle is an electron, and it has no mass. The only thing that changes when a beta particle is emitted is the atomic number of the atom. The atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus, and it is what determines the chemical element of the atom. When a beta particle is emitted, a neutron in the nucleus decays into a proton, an electron, and an antineutrino. The proton remains in the nucleus, but the electron is emitted. This increases the atomic number of the atom by one, but the mass number remains the same.

questions.text: A radioactive element will decay when its atomic number is:

options.explanation: The type of decay that occurs for an element with an atomic number equal to 82 depends on the specific element and its neutron-to-proton ratio.Thus, this option is incorrect because the atomic number should be greater than 82.

The type of decay that occurs for an element with an atomic number less than 82 depends on the specific element and its neutron-to-proton ratio.Thus, this option is incorrect because the atomic number should be greater than 82.

A radioactive element will decay when its atomic number is greater than 82. This is because elements with an atomic number greater than 82 have too many protons in their nucleus, and the repulsive force between the protons becomes too strong. As a result, the nucleus is unstable and will decay, releasing energy in the process.
This option is incorrect because the atomic number should be greater than 82.

questions.text: Which rays have the highest ionization power?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Alpha particles have the highest ionization power of the three main types of ionizing radiation: alpha, beta, and gamma. This is because alpha particles are the most massive and have the highest charge. When an alpha particle collides with an atom, it can knock out multiple electrons, leaving the atom highly ionized.

Beta particles are smaller and lighter than alpha particles, so they have less ionization power. However, beta particles can travel further through matter than alpha particles, so they can be more dangerous if they are ingested or inhaled.

Gamma rays are the least ionizing type of radiation. They are high-energy photons that have no mass or charge. However, gamma rays can travel very long distances through matter, so they can be very dangerous if they are not properly shielded.
The ionization power of X-rays is lower than that of alpha particles and beta particles.

questions.explanation: Alpha particles have the highest ionization power of the three main types of ionizing radiation: alpha, beta, and gamma. This is because alpha particles are the most massive and have the highest charge. When an alpha particle collides with an atom, it can knock out multiple electrons, leaving the atom highly ionized.

The rate of decay depends on the number of nuclei because radioactive decay is a random process. The nuclei in a radioactive sample are constantly decaying, but at any given time, only a small fraction of them will decay. The number of nuclei that decay in a given time period is proportional to the total number of nuclei in the sample.

questions.text: The nuclei have maximum binding energy per nucleon whose mass number lies between:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The nuclei have maximum binding energy per nucleon whose mass number lies between 50 and 60. This is because the nuclear force, which is the force that binds protons and neutrons together in a nucleus, is a short-range force. For nuclei with mass number in this range, the distance between nucleons is not too large, so the nuclear force is strong enough to bind them together. However, for nuclei with mass number outside this range, the distance between nucleons becomes too large, and the nuclear force is not strong enough to bind them together as tightly. As a result, the binding energy per nucleon decreases for nuclei with mass number outside this range.
The distance between nucleons is too large, and the nuclear force is not strong enough to bind them together as tightly. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: The nuclei 2211Na and 2210Ne are known as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons (same atomic number) but different numbers of neutrons (different mass numbers). In the case of isotopes, the element remains the same, but the atomic mass varies.
Isobars are atoms with the same mass number (the sum of protons and neutrons) but different atomic numbers. In this case, the elements are different, but they have the same atomic mass.
Isotones are nuclei with the same number of neutrons but different atomic numbers and, therefore, different elements.

Isomers are nuclei with the same atomic number and mass number but exist in different energy states with different nuclear configurations. They can have different half-lives and radioactive properties.

questions.explanation: In the case of 22Na11 and 22Ne10, they have the same mass number (22) but different atomic numbers (11 for Sodium and 10 for Neon), so they are considered isobars.

questions.text: Which of the following particle has the greatest mass?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electrons are subatomic particles with a very small mass, approximately 9.109 x 10-31 kilograms. They have a negative electric charge.
Positrons are antiparticles of electrons. They have the same mass as electrons but with a positive charge.

Protons are subatomic particles found in the nucleus of atoms. They have a much greater mass than electrons, approximately 1.673 x 10-27 kilograms, making them over 1,800 times more massive than electrons. Protons have a positive electric charge.
Photons are particles of electromagnetic radiation, such as light. They are massless, which means they have zero rest mass. Photons carry energy and momentum but have no rest mass.

questions.explanation: Protons are subatomic particles found in the nucleus of atoms. They have a much greater mass than electrons, approximately 1.673 x 10-27 kilograms, making them over 1,800 times more massive than electrons. Protons have a positive electric charge.

questions.text: Neutron was discovered by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: J.J. Thomson is known for his discovery of the electron, a subatomic particle with a negative charge, through his cathode ray tube experiments. He received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1906 for this discovery.

Sir James Chadwick is the correct answer. He discovered the neutron in 1932, which is a subatomic particle with no charge. This discovery was instrumental in understanding the structure of atomic nuclei and earned Chadwick the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1935.

Ernest Rutherford is known for his contributions to the understanding of the atomic nucleus. He conducted the famous gold foil experiment that led to the discovery of the atomic nucleus and the model of the atom with a central nucleus. However, he did not discover the proton.

Niels Bohr was a Danish physicist known for his contributions to atomic and quantum theory. He developed the Bohr model of the atom, which explained the quantization of electron energy levels. He did not discover the proton.

questions.explanation: Sir James Chadwick is the correct answer. He discovered the neutron in 1932, which is a subatomic particle with no charge. This discovery was instrumental in understanding the structure of atomic nuclei and earned Chadwick the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1935.

questions.text: Half-life of radioactive elements depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The half-life of a radioactive element remains constant and is independent of the amount of the element present. It's a characteristic property of the specific radioactive isotope and is not affected by the quantity of the material.
The half-life of a radioactive element is not affected by pressure. It's a property of the nucleus of the radioactive isotope and is determined by nuclear processes, not external pressure conditions.

The half-life of a radioactive element is also not influenced by temperature. It is a nuclear property related to the probability of decay of individual radioactive atoms and is not significantly affected by changes in temperature.
The half-life of a radioactive element is a constant characteristic of that element, and it is not influenced by the amount of the element, pressure, or temperature.

questions.explanation: Half-life is a natural process that does not depend upon the amount of sample present, pressure, and temperature.

questions.text: If the radioactive decay constant of Radium is 1.07 x 10-4 per year, then its half-life period is approximately equal to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is too high. The calculated value of the half-life of Radium is 6,476 years.

The half-life of a radioactive element is given by the following formula:T1/2 = ln(2) / λwhere T1/2is the half-life, ln(2) is the natural logarithm of 2, and λ is the decay constant. In this case, the decay constant is given as 1.07 x 10-4 per year. So, the half-life of radium is:T1/2 = ln(2) / 1.07 x 10-4 = 6,476 years.
This is too high. The calculated value of the half-life of Radium is 6,476 years.
This is too low. The calculated value of the half-life of Radium is 6,476 years.

questions.text: A detector is exposed to a radioactive source. Fluctuations in the count - rate are observed.What do these fluctuations indicate about radioactive decay?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Fluctuations in the count-rate of a detector exposed to a radioactive source indicate that radioactive decay is a random process. Radioactive decay involves the spontaneous transformation of unstable atomic nuclei into more stable configurations by emitting radiation (such as alpha or beta particles) or photons (gamma rays). These decay events occur randomly in time, and as a result, the count-rate of particles or photons detected at any given moment can vary significantly due to the random nature of the decay process.
Radioactive decay is indeed a spontaneous process, as it occurs naturally in radioactive materials without any external influence.
Radioactive decay follows an exponential decay law in terms of the number of radioactive nuclei remaining over time. The decay rate decreases over time, but the process is still random.

While the rate of radioactive decay is exponential in terms of the number of remaining radioactive nuclei, it may not appear linear when measuring the count rate over time due to the random nature of individual decay events, but this is not the best way to describe radioactive decay.

questions.explanation: Radioactive sources are random in nature i.e. emission of radioactive particles is unpredictable.
